hero.name: Stephen Asatsa, PhD
hero.headline: Evidence-based psychological care and culturally grounded scholarship for people and institutions shaping healthier futures.
hero.subheadline: Dr. Stephen Asatsa is a Kenyan psychologist, senior lecturer, and Head of Department of Psychology at the Catholic University of Eastern Africa with deep experience in teaching, research, mentorship, and professional practice.

aboutShort: Dr. Stephen is a senior Lecturer and Head of Department of Psychology at the Catholic University of Eastern Africa with extensive experience in academic strategy and research. He is an experienced Consultant Psychologist registered and licensed by the Kenya Counselors and Psychologists Board and co-founder of BeautifulMind Consultants, a Kenyan mental health social enterprise.
aboutFull: Dr. Stephen is a senior Lecturer and Head of Department of Psychology at the Catholic University of Eastern Africa with extensive experience in academic strategy and research. Proven track record as a Lecturer of Psychology, excelling in teaching, research, and student mentorship.

He is an experienced Consultant Psychologist registered and licensed by the Kenya Counselors and Psychologists Board and co-founder of BeautifulMind Consultants, a Kenyan mental health social enterprise.

He serves on the governing Council of the Society for Research in Child Development (SRCD). He is the Africa regional representative for the European Association of Personality Psychology (EAPP). He is the e-newsletter editor for the International Society for the Study of Behavioral Development (ISSBD).

He is also a review editor for Frontiers in Psychology and Frontiers in Reproductive Health. His research interests span Indigenous knowledge systems, decolonization of psychology, thanatology, and cultural evolution. He is a strong advocate for the indigenization of psychological practice.

quote.text: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars. Everyone has problems to deal with, but the people who are mesmerized by the beauty of their dreams are the ones who actually live life to the fullest.
quote.author: Oscar Wilde

services.title: Psychotherapy
services.description: Thoughtful support for stress, burnout, grief and loss, trauma, adolescent difficulties, relationship challenges, anxiety, depression, and life transitions.

services.title: Consulting
services.description: Professional psychological consultation for organizations, institutions, and teams navigating change, wellbeing, leadership, and workplace resilience.

services.title: Research Leadership
services.description: Research design, collaboration, and dissemination for interdisciplinary projects rooted in local context and rigorous scholarship.

services.title: Mentorship
services.description: Guidance for students, early-career researchers, and professionals building meaningful careers in psychology and allied disciplines.

services.title: Corporate Training
services.description: Engaging sessions for institutions looking to strengthen mental health literacy, coping skills, and culture-sensitive human support systems.

services.title: Booking Support
services.description: A dedicated consultation flow for people seeking one-on-one guidance and support through an accessible booking process.

researchProjects.title: Traditional Luhya mourning rituals: A cultural evolutionary approach to understanding community wellbeing, cooperation, cultural transmission, and cultural resilience in a Kenyan Indigenous community
researchProjects.summary: A major interdisciplinary project exploring the therapeutic value of Indigenous mourning rituals, their role in cooperation, and how they support cultural transmission and resilience in western Kenya.
researchProjects.funding: Templeton Foundation and Cultural Evolution Society
details: Grief after the loss of a loved one is a universal human emotion. Communities worldwide have developed mourning rituals to bring closure to the bereaved while reintegrating them into the community.
Indigenous mourning rituals face ongoing threats from globalizing forces, yet limited attention has been paid to how these rituals foster individual and community wellbeing and resilience.
Our project investigates how globalization has affected the practice and transmission of Indigenous mourning rituals from the Luhya community of western Kenya.

We specifically aim to answer three interrelated questions: (1) What is the therapeutic value of Indigenous mourning rituals? (2) Do mourning rituals promote cooperation? and (3) How does participation in mourning rituals contribute to cultural transmission?

Using qualitative and quantitative methods including focus groups, interviews, behavioral observations, and psychometric tests with traditional healers, Christian leaders, and bereaved adolescents and adults.
Findings will be incorporated into training curricula and policy briefs to promote the valuation of Indigenous practices among Kenyan mental health practitioners.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: The Catholic University of Eastern Africa, Kenya
team.bio: Stephen Asatsa, PhD, is the Principal Investigator leading this interdisciplinary research on Indigenous mourning rituals and cultural resilience.

team.name: Sheina Lew-Levy
team.bio: Sheina Lew-Levy is an assistant professor of Psychology at Durham University. She holds a PhD in Psychology from the University of Cambridge (2019). Drawing from anthropological and psychological theory, she conducts research in hunter-gatherer societies to understand the cultural diversity in, and evolution of, social learning in childhood. Specifically, she has used quantitative and qualitative methods to study how and from whom BaYaka and Hadza children from Congo and Tanzania, respectively, learn through meaningful participation in everyday activities. As the co-founder and co-director of Forager Child Studies, she also conducts cross-cultural reviews and secondary data analysis on the pasts, presents, and futures of hunter-gatherer children's learning.

team.name: Eric J Ringen
team.bio: Erik is the Consultant Statistician for the project. He is an expert in Bayesian bespoke multilevel statistical modeling. He will assist the research team in developing computational workflows in R to clean and analyze our data. He will also ensure that our statistical modeling approach is appropriate to our research question, and that we are interpreting results effectively.

researchProjects.title: The Africa Long Life Study: A longitudinal exploration of the psychological development of young adults in Kenya, Namibia and South Africa
researchProjects.summary: A five-year longitudinal collaborative study examining personality traits, mental health, and the psychological development of emerging adults across multiple African contexts.
researchProjects.funding: Swiss National Science Foundation and the University of Zurich
details: This is a longitudinal collaborative study examining the psychological development of emerging adults with focus on personality traits and mental disorders with data being collected over a period of 5 years.

The work is spearheaded by psychologists from the University of Zurich, Switzerland; The Catholic University of Eastern Africa, Kenya; University of Namibia and University of the Free State, South Africa.

The Kenyan sample consists of eight hundred (800) 18-year-olds at the point of the first wave of data collection, of which 50% are men and 50% are women, from throughout Kenya and belonging to any ethno-linguistic group.

Data is collected at intervals of six months for a period of 5 years. The Kenya research team is supported by a team of postgraduate students from the Catholic University of Eastern Africa working as research assistants.

The goals of the study are to compare results to those from other contexts, in order to help differentiate more universal from culturally specific aspects of psychological phenomena among emerging adults.
The findings will inform counsellors, policy makers and educators in different countries about the characteristics and needs of this generation of young adults.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: Department of Psychology, The Catholic University of Eastern Africa
team.bio: Dr. Stephen Asatsa leads the Kenyan component of this landmark longitudinal study on emerging adult development across Africa.

team.name: Amber Gayle Thalmayer
team.affiliation: Department of Psychology, University of Zürich
team.bio: Professor Dr. Amber Gayle Thalmayer is the overall principal investigator in all the involved countries. Assistant Professor for Personality, Mental Health, and Culture at the University of Zürich.

team.name: Elizabeth Shino
team.affiliation: Department Of Psychology, University of Namibia
team.bio: Dr. Elizabeth Shino leads the Namibian component of the Africa Long Life Study, bringing expertise in cross-cultural developmental psychology.

team.bio: Professor Luzelle Naudé leads the South African component of the study, contributing extensive expertise in developmental psychology and mental health research.

researchProjects.title: Impact of Covid 19 Experiences on Emotional Regulation among Adolescents in 6 Countries
researchProjects.summary: A cross-country study investigating how Covid-19 disruptions affected religiosity, character strengths, and emotional regulation among adolescents in Kenya, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Namibia, the Philippines, and Australia.
researchProjects.funding: Templeton Foundation and the International Consortium of Developmental Science Societies
details: The onset of Covid-19 brought about disruption in almost every sector globally, leading to travel restrictions, and the closure of schools, workplaces, and places of worship.

Meaning-making and religion have emerged as one means for developing coping skills to manage stress. When the ability to use those tools is disrupted, adolescents are likely to revert to maladaptive attempts to cope.
In the education sector, school shutdowns resulted in the loss of a full academic year, affecting about 168 million children globally (UNICEF, 2021).

During the recovery period, Kenya witnessed heightened delinquency among school adolescents in the form of arson, indicating a shift in emotional regulation strategies and character strengths of adolescents.
This study hypothesizes that Covid-19 disruptions inversely affected religiosity and character strengths, consequently reducing their buffering effect against stress.
The study examines the mediating role of religiosity and character strengths on emotional regulation among adolescents during the Covid-19 pandemic period in 6 countries.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: Department of Psychology, The Catholic University of Eastern Africa
team.bio: Dr. Stephen Asatsa leads the Kenyan component of this international study on adolescent emotional regulation during the COVID-19 pandemic.

team.name: Alex Gardener
team.bio: Alex Gardener leads the Australian component of the study, bringing expertise in adolescent mental health and emotional regulation.

team.name: Eden Terol
team.bio: Eden Terol leads the Philippine component of the study, contributing expertise in adolescent psychology and pandemic impacts.

team.name: Mulat Asnake Goshu
team.bio: Mulat Asnake Goshu leads the Ethiopian component of the study, bringing expertise in adolescent mental health and cultural factors.

team.name: Chidozie Nwafor
team.bio: Chidozie Nwafor leads the Nigerian component of the study, contributing expertise in adolescent emotional regulation and cultural psychology.

researchProjects.title: Covid-19 Pandemic-Related Impacts and Severity of Substance Use Disorder Symptoms among Patients in Selected Rehabilitation Centers in Nairobi, Kenya
researchProjects.summary: Research on how pandemic containment measures shaped addiction severity and treatment conditions among residential rehabilitation patients in Nairobi.
researchProjects.funding: International Consortium of Universities for Drug Demand Reduction (ICUDDR)
details: The onset of Covid-19 brought about disruption in almost every sector globally. In an attempt to contain the spread of the virus, many countries imposed travel restrictions, closed social places, and introduced working from home.

Among the affected places in Kenya were rehabilitation centers, where patients were discharged to avoid infection. This was accompanied by the closure of bars and the introduction of curfews, among other measures.

Data on the possible impact of these disruptions on substance use disorders remained scant. This study aimed to examine the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on the severity of substance use disorder symptoms to inform treatment programs during the ongoing pandemic.

The study was guided by objectives: to assess addiction severity before and during the Covid-19 pandemic among patients in selected rehabilitation centers; to examine the effect of the pandemic on patients; and to explore the relationship between Covid-19 containment measures and the severity of addiction.

The study targeted 204 residential patients in selected rehabilitation centers in Nairobi, who were selected through simple random sampling, with the inclusion criterion being those who had started treatment before the Covid-19 pandemic.
Data were collected using a sociodemographic questionnaire, the Epidemic-Pandemic Impacts Inventory (EPII), and the Modified Addiction Severity Index.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: Department of Psychology, The Catholic University of Eastern Africa
team.bio: Dr. Stephen Asatsa leads this important study on substance use disorder impacts during the COVID-19 pandemic in Nairobi rehabilitation centers.

researchProjects.title: Global research on work addiction
researchProjects.summary: Contribution to international work on macro-, meso-, and micro-level drivers of work addiction and associated health outcomes.
details: This stream focuses on the social and structural dimensions of work addiction and related health problems.
The project examines macro-level factors (economic policies, workplace culture), meso-level factors (organizational practices), and micro-level factors (individual differences) in work addiction.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: The Catholic University of Eastern Africa, Kenya
team.bio: Dr. Stephen Asatsa contributes to this international research on work addiction, bringing expertise from the African context.

researchProjects.title: Global Collaboration on Traumatic Stress
researchProjects.summary: International work on culture-sensitive trauma assessment and the adaptation of trauma research across contexts.
details: The collaboration centers on culturally sensitive assessment frameworks in psychotrauma research.
This work addresses the need for trauma assessment tools that are culturally appropriate and valid across different contexts and populations.

team.name: Stephen Asatsa
team.affiliation: The Catholic University of Eastern Africa, Kenya
team.bio: Dr. Stephen Asatsa contributes to this global collaboration on traumatic stress, bringing expertise in cultural psychology and trauma from the African context.

testimonials.name: Prof. Luzelle Naudé
testimonials.quote: Stephen brings clarity, compassion, and intellectual depth to both research and mentorship. He is a thoughtful collaborator with a strong grounding in culturally meaningful scholarship.

testimonials.name: Dr. Elizabeth Shino
testimonials.quote: His work bridges practice and academia in a way that feels rigorous, humane, and deeply relevant to African realities.

testimonials.name: Research Mentee
